title: Contributing to GLBFP
text: Thank considering contributing GLBFP! 🎉 welcome contributions, whether ’s fixing bug, improving documentation, adding new features.

title: Contributing to GLBFP
text: Click Fork button top right repository create copy GitHub account.

title: Contributing to GLBFP
text: Download forked repository local machine: Replace AurelienNicosiaUlaval actual GitHub username.
code: git clone https://github.com/AurelienNicosiaUlaval/GLBFP.git cd GLBFP

title: Contributing to GLBFP
text: making changes, create new branch: Use descriptive name branch.
code: git checkout -b feature-your-feature-name

title: Contributing to GLBFP
text: Add edit functions R/ directory. Update documentation necessary (README.md man/ folder). Run tests (devtools::test()) ensure everything works.

title: Contributing to GLBFP
text: changes ready, commit :
code: git add . git commit -m "Brief description of the changes"

title: Contributing to GLBFP
text: Upload changes GitHub fork:
code: git push origin feature-your-feature-name

title: Contributing to GLBFP
text: Go original repository click New Pull Request. Select fork branch, describe changes.

title: Contributing to GLBFP
text: Follow tidyverse style guide R code. Document new functions using roxygen2 format. Ensure tests pass submitting PR. respectful collaborative.

title: Contributing to GLBFP
text: find bug want request new feature, please open issue GitHub Issues. appreciate help improving GLBFP! 🚀

title: Introduction to GLBFP
text: GLBFP provides nonparametric density estimators based histogram-style constructions remain computationally light interpretable. Use cases: fast exploratory density estimation, smooth alternatives raw histograms, 1D 2D density estimation consistent API.

title: Introduction to GLBFP
text: ASH() estimates one point. LBFP() estimates one point linear blending. GLBFP() extends LBFP shift parameter m. *_estimate() runs estimators grid.

title: Introduction to GLBFP
code: x1 <- matrix(rnorm(300), ncol = 1) b1 <- compute_bi_optim(x1, m = 1)  fit1 <- GLBFP(x = 0, data = x1, b = b1, m = 1) fit1 #> GLBFP Density Estimation: #> Point: (0)  #> Estimated density: 0.3867351  #> Estimated standard error: 0.0838592  #> 95% confidence interval: 0.362643259199835, 0.410826868862035  #> Bandwidths (b): 0.155144970240404  #> Shifts (m): 1  grid1 <- GLBFP_estimate(data = x1, b = b1, m = 1, grid_size = 100) plot(grid1)

title: Introduction to GLBFP
code: x2 <- ashua[, c("flow", "level")] b2 <- c(8, 0.4)  fit2 <- GLBFP(x = c(mean(x2$flow), mean(x2$level)), data = x2, b = b2, m = c(1, 1)) fit2 #> GLBFP Density Estimation: #> Point: (249.022601959444, 30.4196864889496)  #> Estimated density: 0.003774417  #> Estimated standard error: 0.0003167353  #> 95% confidence interval: 0.00376917856755285, 0.00377965508177405  #> Bandwidths (b): 8, 0.4  #> Shifts (m): 1, 1  grid2 <- GLBFP_estimate(data = x2, b = b2, m = c(1, 1), grid_size = 20) plot(grid2, contour = TRUE)

title: Introduction to GLBFP
text: Start compute_bi_optim(data, m = rep(1, d)), tune around value context.

title: Introduction to GLBFP
text: Use small integers first (e.g. 1 2). Larger values increase smoothing compute time.

title: Introduction to GLBFP
text: Missing values (NA) accepted. small samples can produce unstable tails. custom grid_points, irregular grids allowed 3D surfaces interpretable rectangular grids.

title: GLBFP validation and comparison
text: benchmark intentionally small keep vignette runtime short. Increase reps grid sizes inst/bench/run_validation.R paper-grade tables.

title: Getting started with GLBFP
code: install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_github("AurelienNicosiaULaval/GLBFP")

title: Getting started with GLBFP
code: library(GLBFP)

title: Getting started with GLBFP
text: functions ASH(), LBFP(), GLBFP() estimate density single point. data supplied numeric matrix data frame observations rows. returned object contains evaluation point, estimated density, bandwidth, shift parameter. LBFP() GLBFP(), uncertainty components also returned.
code: x <- matrix(rnorm(300), ncol = 1) b <- compute_bi_optim(x, m = 1)  fit <- GLBFP(x = 0, data = x, b = b, m = 1) fit #> GLBFP Density Estimation: #> Point: (0)  #> Estimated density: 0.3867351  #> Estimated standard error: 0.0838592  #> 95% confidence interval: 0.362643259199835, 0.410826868862035  #> Bandwidths (b): 0.155144970240404  #> Shifts (m): 1 names(fit) #> [1] "x"          "estimation" "sd"         "IC"         "b"          #> [6] "m"          "method"     "dimension" summary(fit) #> Method: GLBFP  #> Dimension: 1  #> Point: 0  #> Estimation: 0.3867351  #> Standard error: 0.0838592  #> 95% CI: 0.362643259199835, 0.410826868862035 predict(fit) #> [1] 0.3867351

title: Getting started with GLBFP
text: *_estimate() functions evaluate estimator regular grid user-supplied set points. one-dimensional grids, plot method returns ggplot2 object.

title: Getting started with GLBFP
text: ashua data contain daily flow level observations Ashuapmushuan river. example uses small grid vignette remains fast.
code: data("ashua")  river_data <- ashua[, c("flow", "level")] b2 <- c(8, 0.4) x0 <- c(mean(river_data$flow), mean(river_data$level))  fit2 <- GLBFP(x = x0, data = river_data, b = b2, m = c(1, 1)) fit2 #> GLBFP Density Estimation: #> Point: (249.022601959444, 30.4196864889496)  #> Estimated density: 0.003774417  #> Estimated standard error: 0.0003167353  #> 95% confidence interval: 0.00376917856755285, 0.00377965508177405  #> Bandwidths (b): 8, 0.4  #> Shifts (m): 1, 1  grid_fit2 <- GLBFP_estimate(   data = river_data,   b = b2,   m = c(1, 1),   grid_size = 15 )  plot(grid_fit2, contour = TRUE)

title: Getting started with GLBFP
text: package expects finite numeric data. Missing values removed imputed estimation. Constant data require explicit non-degenerate min_vals max_vals bounds density estimator continuous data needs positive evaluation range.

title: Brief methodological background
text: Histogram-based estimators approximate density aggregating observations bins. Frequency polygon estimators smooth histogram idea linear interpolation neighboring bin heights. Averaged shifted histograms use several shifted grids average resulting estimates. ideas discussed standard references multivariate density estimation, including Scott (1992), work nonparametric density estimation bounds Terrell Scott (1985).

title: Brief methodological background
text: ASH() computes averaged shifted histogram estimate point. LBFP() computes linear blend frequency polygon estimate point. GLBFP() computes general linear blend frequency polygon estimate point, using shift vector m. corresponding *_estimate() functions evaluate estimators grid. intended visualization reproducible numerical summaries.

title: Brief methodological background
text: bandwidth vector b controls scale bins. Smaller values can increase local variation, larger values can smooth estimate. helper compute_bi_optim() provides plug-starting value. shift vector m must contain positive integers. Larger values increase number shifted components therefore increase computational cost.

title: Brief methodological background
text: package provides R implementation, documentation, examples, tests, benchmark scaffolding GLBFP workflows. introduce new theoretical result.

title: Brief methodological background
text: Scott, D. W. (1992). Multivariate Density Estimation: Theory, Practice, Visualization. Wiley. doi:10.1002/9780470316849. Terrell, G. R., Scott, D. W. (1985). Oversmoothed Nonparametric Density Estimates. Journal American Statistical Association, 80(389), 209-214. doi:10.1080/01621459.1985.10477163. complete bibliographic record original GLBFP methodological article yet verified repository. added using vignette source material journal article.

title: Two-dimensional density estimation
text: example uses small reproducible mixture vignette remains quick build.
code: n <- 250 group <- rbinom(n, size = 1, prob = 0.5)  x <- cbind(   rnorm(n, mean = ifelse(group == 1, -1.2, 1.2), sd = 0.7),   rnorm(n, mean = ifelse(group == 1, 1.0, -1.0), sd = 0.8) )  colnames(x) <- c("x1", "x2")  b <- compute_bi_optim(x, m = c(1, 1)) b #> [1] 0.3210361 0.3159536

title: Two-dimensional density estimation
code: x0 <- c(0, 0)  ash_fit <- ASH(x0, x, b = b, m = c(1, 1)) lbfp_fit <- LBFP(x0, x, b = b) glbfp_fit <- GLBFP(x0, x, b = b, m = c(1, 1))  c(   ASH = ash_fit$estimation,   LBFP = lbfp_fit$estimation,   GLBFP = glbfp_fit$estimation ) #>        ASH       LBFP      GLBFP  #> 0.03943508 0.01941260 0.01941260

title: Two-dimensional density estimation
code: grid_fit <- GLBFP_estimate(x, b = b, m = c(1, 1), grid_size = 20) summary(grid_fit) #> Method: GLBFP  #> Dimension: 2  #> Grid points: 400  #> Density range: 0 to 0.210331281836707  #> Density median: 0.003486427  #> Density mean: 0.02480127

title: Two-dimensional density estimation
text: two-dimensional regular grids, contour = TRUE returns static ggplot2 contour plot.  contour = FALSE, plot method returns interactive plotly surface. useful exploration, static contours usually easier reproduce manuscripts.
code: plot(grid_fit, contour = TRUE) surface <- plot(grid_fit, contour = FALSE) surface

title: Two-dimensional density estimation
text: number evaluation points grows quickly grid_size two dimensions. manuscript figures, start moderate grid increase confirming visual conclusions stable.

title: Authors and Citation
text: Aurélien Nicosia. Author, maintainer.

title: Authors and Citation
text: Nicosia (2026). GLBFP: General Linear Blend Frequency Polygon Density Estimation. doi:10.5281/zenodo.17945962. R package version 0.5.1, https://github.com/AurelienNicosiaULaval/GLBFP.
code: @Manual{,   title = {GLBFP: General Linear Blend Frequency Polygon Density Estimation},   author = {Aurélien Nicosia},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 0.5.1},   url = {https://github.com/AurelienNicosiaULaval/GLBFP},   doi = {10.5281/zenodo.17945962}, }

what: General Linear Blend Frequency Polygon Density Estimation
title: General Linear Blend Frequency Polygon Density Estimation
text: GLBFP provides R implementations histogram-based nonparametric density estimators: Averaged Shifted Histogram: ASH(), ASH_estimate() Linear Blend Frequency Polygon: LBFP(), LBFP_estimate() General Linear Blend Frequency Polygon: GLBFP(), GLBFP_estimate() package focuses pointwise estimation, grid-based estimation, 1D 2D visualization, reproducible examples suitable package documentation software-paper preparation.

title: General Linear Blend Frequency Polygon Density Estimation
text: package active development currently CRAN. current development target CRAN readiness preparation software article R Journal.

title: General Linear Blend Frequency Polygon Density Estimation
text: Install development version GitHub: package later accepted CRAN, installation use:
code: install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_github("AurelienNicosiaULaval/GLBFP") install.packages("GLBFP")

title: General Linear Blend Frequency Polygon Density Estimation
code: library(GLBFP)  set.seed(2026) x <- matrix(rnorm(300), ncol = 1) b <- compute_bi_optim(x, m = 1)  fit <- GLBFP(x = 0, data = x, b = b, m = 1) fit

title: General Linear Blend Frequency Polygon Density Estimation
code: library(GLBFP)  data("ashua")  river_data <- ashua[, c("flow", "level")] b <- c(8, 0.4) x0 <- c(mean(river_data$flow), mean(river_data$level))  fit <- GLBFP(x = x0, data = river_data, b = b, m = c(1, 1)) fit  grid_fit <- GLBFP_estimate(   data = river_data,   b = b,   m = c(1, 1),   grid_size = 20 )  plot(grid_fit, contour = TRUE)

title: General Linear Blend Frequency Polygon Density Estimation
text: package includes short, reproducible vignettes : getting started; brief methodological background; two-dimensional density estimation; validation benchmark scenarios.

title: General Linear Blend Frequency Polygon Density Estimation
text: General background frequency polygons, averaged shifted histograms, multivariate density estimation available : Scott, D. W. (1992). Multivariate Density Estimation: Theory, Practice, Visualization. Wiley. doi:10.1002/9780470316849. Terrell, G. R., Scott, D. W. (1985). Oversmoothed Nonparametric Density Estimates. Journal American Statistical Association, 80(389), 209-214. doi:10.1080/01621459.1985.10477163. complete bibliographic record original GLBFP methodological article yet verified repository. tracked dev/references_to_verify.md added journal submission.

title: Compute the \(G(m_i)\) bandwidth constant — G_i
text: Computes scalar constant \(G(m_i)\) used compute_bi_optim().

title: Compute the \(G(m_i)\) bandwidth constant — G_i
code: G_i(mi)

title: Compute the \(G(m_i)\) bandwidth constant — G_i
text: mi single positive numeric value. package estimators, mi corresponds one component integer shift vector m.

title: Compute the \(G(m_i)\) bandwidth constant — G_i
text: positive numeric scalar.

title: Compute the \(G(m_i)\) bandwidth constant — G_i
text: implemented formula $$G(m_i) = \frac{1}{12}\left(1 + \frac{1}{2m_i^2}\right).$$

title: Compute the \(K(m_i)\) bandwidth constant — K_mi
text: Computes scalar constant \(K(m_i)\) used compute_bi_optim().

title: Compute the \(K(m_i)\) bandwidth constant — K_mi
code: K_mi(mi)

title: Compute the \(K(m_i)\) bandwidth constant — K_mi
text: mi single numeric value greater 0.5. package estimators, mi corresponds one component integer shift vector m.

title: Compute the \(K(m_i)\) bandwidth constant — K_mi
text: positive numeric scalar.

title: Compute the \(K(m_i)\) bandwidth constant — K_mi
text: implemented formula $$ K(m_i) = \sqrt{\frac{1}{6} + \frac{1}{12m_i^2}} + \frac{4m_i^2 - 1}{6\sqrt{2}m_i} \log\left( \frac{\sqrt{3} + \sqrt{4m_i^2 + 2}}{\sqrt{4m_i^2 - 1}} \right). $$

title: Compute the \(G^*\) bandwidth constant — compute_G_star
text: Computes dimension-dependent constant \(G^*\) used compute_bi_optim().

title: Compute the \(G^*\) bandwidth constant — compute_G_star
code: compute_G_star(d)

title: Compute the \(G^*\) bandwidth constant — compute_G_star
text: d single positive integer giving data dimension.

title: Compute the \(G^*\) bandwidth constant — compute_G_star
text: positive numeric scalar.

title: Compute the \(G^*\) bandwidth constant — compute_G_star
text: implemented formula $$G^* = 2^{\frac{3(d - 4)}{2(4 + d)}} \exp\left(\frac{1}{4 + d}\right) \frac{\pi^{d / 2}}{4 + d}.$$

title: Compute bandwidth vector \(b_i\) — compute_bi_optim
text: Computes plug-bandwidth vector used GLBFP/LBFP/ASH estimators. function validates numeric inputs, stabilizes near-singular covariance matrices small ridge needed, returns strictly positive bandwidths.

title: Compute bandwidth vector \(b_i\) — compute_bi_optim
code: compute_bi_optim(data, m = rep(1, ncol(data)))

title: Compute bandwidth vector \(b_i\) — compute_bi_optim
text: data numeric matrix data frame rows observations columns variables. m positive integer vector shifts, one value per dimension.

title: Compute bandwidth vector \(b_i\) — compute_bi_optim
text: numeric vector positive bandwidths one value per column data.

title: Compute bandwidth vector \(b_i\) — compute_bi_optim
text: returned vector intended starting value examples routine workflows. applied analysis, sensitivity bandwidth still checked. Near-singular covariance matrices stabilized small ridge term. fails, function returns error rather silently producing non-finite bandwidths.

title: Compute bandwidth vector \(b_i\) — compute_bi_optim
code: set.seed(1) x <- cbind(rnorm(200), rnorm(200)) compute_bi_optim(x, m = c(1, 1)) #> [1] 0.3109093 0.3153027

title: GLBFP 0.5.1
text: Added test infrastructure (testthat edition 3) regression/invariant tests. Added S3 helpers summary() predict() fit/grid objects. Added two publication-oriented vignettes (introduction validation). Added reproducible benchmark scripts benchmarks/ validation helpers inst/bench. Added pkgdown coverage workflows. Added citation files (CITATION.cff, inst/CITATION) cran-comments.md. Added pre-R Journal preparation files dev/ rjournal-prep/.

title: GLBFP 0.5.1
text: Strengthened input validation (finite numeric checks, shape checks, explicit errors). Improved numerical robustness compute_bi_optim() near-singular covariance matrices. Fixed plotting methods irregular custom grids metadata handling. Fixed broken references plot.ASH_estimate().

title: GLBFP 0.4.0
text: Added standard deviation confidence interval output *_estimate functions.

title: GLBFP 0.3.0
text: Internal maintenance release.

title: GLBFP 0.2.0
text: Fixed errors ASH() GLBFP().

title: GLBFP 0.1.0
text: Initial release.
